headline: Net ROAS jumped from 59% to 96% (+37 pts) on 44% less spend — net loss narrowed from −$1.38M to −$75K, nearly reaching breakeven. AOV up 44% ($204 → $294) while orders fell 43%: a deliberate trade of volume for efficiency.
physical_takeaways: RS1 is the new anchor — a new product that didn't exist in June 2025. It's now the largest physical funnel by revenue.
357 held volume well. AOV increased from $245 to est. $360 following a price increase from $199 to $249.
SF1 is offline — 3,064 FE sales and $889K in June 2025, zero this June. SF2 was built as its replacement and has absorbed that load.
AOV story is real: prior year blended AOV was $237. Current year estimated at $378 — a 60% jump driven by RS1 and the 357 price increase.
SQSE/SQP/TOW/THR accounted for 3,460 sales in June 2025. These funnels were taken offline because they could not hit Net ROAS targets — the volume loss is intentional, not structural.
digital_takeaways: SSTS collapsed: 3,032 sales in June 2025 to 285 in June 2026 (-91%). Single biggest volume loss in digital.
WPSS ($110K, $198 AOV) is new this year — didn't exist in June 2025. Partially offsets SSTS volume decline with higher AOV.
OSSF held steady: 885 to 877 FE sales (flat), AOV rose from $96 to $112. OP CVR dropped from 73% to 62%.
SRSW scaled from 53 to 339 FE sales (+540%), revenue from $4K to $31K. Small base but strong growth trajectory.
PSS became WPSS in 2026 — WPSS recorded 556 FE sales and $110K revenue in June at $198 AOV, a significantly higher price point than PSS's prior $135 AOV.

pg1_shopify_trials.note: ⚠ Pending — trial definition changed multiple times; pull from testing dashboard once stable

pg1_coc_trials.note: Deprioritized — moving off CoC (357 and digital funnels still on CoC, migrating to Shopify)

tests_concluded.name: One Shot Slice Fix — Checkout Champ
Club Only + Club + App Copy (3 variants) — Shopify
ONE.1 Wedge — Help Me Choose Quiz — Shopify
tests_concluded.revenue_stat: RPV −20% (non-primary · AOV skewed by quiz path)

tests_concluded.name: V2 Club Only + Club + App — Shopify-wide (Var A)
tests_concluded.revenue_stat: AOV +4% · Membership shift +25pp · CVR collapse offset all AOV gains

tests_concluded.name: V2 Club Only + Club + App — Shopify-wide (Var B)
tests_concluded.revenue_stat: AOV +13% · Membership shift +32pp · RPV still negative

tests_concluded.revenue_stat: AOV +9% · Membership shift +34pp · Max CPC compressed $2.26 → $1.58

tests_concluded.revenue_stat: Help Me Choose clicked: 2.1% · ran Jun 8–23 during heavy promo

themes.title: Efficiency over volume — the business chose margin
themes.narrative: Spend dropped 46% and orders dropped 43%, but Net ROAS jumped from 44% to 111% and refund rate collapsed from 32% to 7%. The shift away from high-volume, high-refund digital funnels (led by SSG2 being retired) drove the biggest efficiency gain in recent memory.
themes.takeaway: Protect this efficiency — don't let spend creep back into low-quality volume.

themes.title: AOV is the growth lever that's working
themes.narrative: Physical AOV: +46% ($259 → $378). Digital AOV: +82% ($258 → $470). Higher-ticket funnels (RS1 at $400, WPSS at $198) are replacing the commodity volume. The business is moving upmarket.
themes.takeaway: Double down on high-AOV funnels. Any new test should have an AOV hypothesis.

themes.title: PG1 v3 on Shopify is the structural bet
themes.narrative: Moving PG1 to Shopify and introducing a 7-day no-subscription free trial removes the forced continuity model that created refund exposure. Removing misleading checkout text (implying subscription when it was a free trial) addresses a trust and compliance issue at the highest-volume point in the funnel.
themes.takeaway: Free trial clarity at checkout is a CRO win and a retention investment — measure trial-to-paid conversion as the north star.

triumphs: Launched PG1 v3 on Shopify with a 7-day no-sub free trial — moving off forced continuity toward an opt-in model.
Removed misleading checkout text that implied customers were getting a subscription when they were actually starting a free trial.
11 tests concluded in June — 2 wins, 6 losses, 3 flat. 0546 (357 CoC) and 0545 (OSSF CoC) both shipped.
challenges: Free trial tracking is unreliable — the definition has changed multiple times, making it impossible to get a clean total count or a trustworthy trial-to-paid conversion rate. Need a single agreed-upon definition locked in before we can measure the PG1 v3 launch properly.

Trial-to-paid conversion rate is not yet measurable by platform — CoC and Shopify handle trials differently, and we can't compare them apples-to-apples until reporting is separated and standardized per platform.

pricing_model_analysis.headline: Pricing Model Analysis — V0 through V3
pricing_model_analysis.sub: Four pricing eras · Mar 6 through Jul 11 · V0 is the forced-continuity baseline (not a viable path) · Source: DOMO Ad Performance

versions.label: Forced Continuity — Baseline
versions.description: Pre-change baseline. $29/mo PG1 subscription auto-added and forced at checkout. High trial volume but driven by force, not intent. Not a viable path — compliance and refund risk required moving away from this model.

versions.label: Member / Non-Member Pricing
versions.description: Baseline. Two price points — members saw a lower price, non-members a higher price. No trial offer. This was the live structure before any testing began.

versions.label: Club & Club + App Pricing
versions.description: Same price point as V1 but reframed as two options: Club only vs. Club + App. A/B tests (PG08, PG14) ran this structure against the V1 member/non-member baseline — every variant returned a CVR loss.

versions.label: Single Price + Auto-Trial
versions.description: Removed the two-option structure entirely. Single checkout price across all funnels. PG1 7-day free trial auto-added to cart (removable). Live Jun 10 Shopify, Jun 11 357 CC. First profitable period of all three.

pricing_model_analysis.projection_headline: What if V2 had been fully implemented at V3 spend scale?
pricing_model_analysis.projection_sub: Projecting V2's observed CVR (0.83%) and trial attach rate (57.9%) onto V3's actual click volume (1,120,793 clicks at $1.8M spend).
pricing_model_analysis.callout_positive: V3 wins on every dimension that matters. CVR nearly recovered to V1 (0.92% vs 0.95%). Net ROAS crossed into profit for the first time at 103%. CPA is declining at $175. And subscription acquisition reached its highest rate ever — 75 trials per 100 orders — without any pricing friction. V3 at $1.8M spend is producing +1,265 more orders, +$268K more gross revenue, and +2,581 more PG1 trials than V2 would have at the same scale. Net revenue swing: -$81K projected → +$61K actual = $142K flip.
pricing_model_analysis.callout_watch: AOV is down from V2 ($306 → $295). V2's non-member price premium drove higher AOV; V3's single price brought it back down slightly. At 75.4% trial attach rate and $29/month, even 2-month average subscriber retention more than covers the $11 AOV gap per buyer — but trial-to-paid conversion rate is the metric to watch in the next 30–60 days.

ab_test_evidence.headline: A/B test evidence behind the V2 reversal
ab_test_evidence.intro: Six tests across SF2 (Shopify) and Shopify-wide funnels compared the Club & Club + App two-option structure (V2) against the member/non-member baseline (V1). Every variant returned an RPV loss — meaning the Club/Club + App reframe hurt revenue per visitor despite AOV holding up, because CVR dropped more than price could compensate.

sub_ltv_math.headline: Subscription LTV math: why V2 didn't pencil
sub_ltv_math.conclusion: Even at full subscription ramp, V2 (Club & Club + App) required subscribers to stay for 10.3 months on average to offset the revenue lost from the CVR drop vs. V1 (member/non-member). Industry churn rates make that a very long bet — and there was no data suggesting PG1 retention would hold at that level.

paid_media_impact.headline: Paid media double penalty: bid ceiling + CVR
paid_media_impact.intro: The Club & Club + App structure (V2) created two simultaneous headwinds vs. the member/non-member baseline (V1). First, RPV dropped on every variant, compressing the max efficient CPC — the algorithm could no longer bid as aggressively without breaking ROAS targets. Second, the CVR drop meant more clicks (and spend) were required per purchase. Either alone is manageable; together they make scaling unprofitable.
paid_media_impact.conclusion: At V2's max efficient CPC (~$1.29–$1.58 vs $2.04–$2.33 in control), the algorithm had to either accept worse ROAS or pull back spend. V2 chose to scale spend anyway — from $982K in V1 to $1,625K — and the ROAS deterioration confirmed the algorithm was operating outside its efficient range.

path_projection.headline: Path projection: what each version looks like at $1.8M spend
path_projection.sub: Applying each era's observed CVR, AOV, and trial attach rate to V3's actual click volume (1,120,793 clicks at $1,806K spend). V3 actual numbers are real; V1 and V2 are projections.

scenarios.label: Member / Non-Member pricing continues
scenarios.verdict: Near breakeven — no path to profit at scale
scenarios.note: Two price points, no trial. CVR holds but no subscription acquisition engine and no structural change to the checkout experience. ROAS stays near breakeven indefinitely with no LTV lever to pull.

scenarios.label: Club & Club + App fully implemented
scenarios.verdict: Unprofitable with no near-term path to breakeven
scenarios.note: Same price as V1, reframed as Club vs. Club + App. CVR loss confirmed across all 6 tests. Subscription revenue ramps to ~$46.5K/month at steady state but can only close 41% of the revenue gap. Breakeven requires 10.3-month average subscriber retention.

scenarios.label: Single price + auto-trial (actual)
scenarios.verdict: Only path that is profitable from day one
scenarios.note: CVR nearly recovered to V1 levels (0.92% vs 0.95%). Auto-trial drives 75 trial starts per 100 orders — highest attach rate of all three eras — without a price premium or CVR penalty. Profitable at current scale with room to grow spend further.

path_projection.conclusion: V1 was a ceiling — near-breakeven forever with no subscription lever. V2 was a bet on subscriber retention that the LTV math couldn't support. V3 is the only scenario that produces profit from launch, scales the subscription base efficiently, and gives the paid media algorithm a clean ROAS signal to bid against.
